stem: The region \( R \) is bounded by the sideways parabola \( x=y^{2} \) and the line \( x=[[m]]y+([[c]]) \). Since both boundaries are most naturally written as functions of \( y \), the correct setup integrates with respect to \( y \) (not \( x \)). Find the area of \( R \).
solution: The curves meet where \( y^{2}=[[m]]y+([[c]]) \), i.e. at \( y=[[r1]] \) and \( y=[[r2]] \). Setting up the correct integral with respect to \( y \), the area is \( \int_{[[r1]]}^{[[r2]]}\left(([[m]]y+([[c]])) - y^{2}\right)dy = [[answer]] \). (Setting this up in \( x \) instead would require splitting into two pieces and solving for \( y \) as a multivalued function of \( x \) — far more work.)
distractor_rationales: This is the square of the vertical extent between the intersection points, not the enclosed area.
This integrates bottom minus top instead of top minus bottom, giving the negative of the area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: The region under the curve \( y=[[m]]x^{2} \), for \( 0 \le x \le [[k]] \), is revolved about the \( y \)-axis. The correct washer setup integrates with respect to \( y \), using outer radius \( x=[[k]] \) and inner radius \( x=\sqrt{y/[[m]]} \) for \( 0\le y\le [[ytop]] \). Find the volume of the solid generated.
solution: With \( x=\sqrt{y/[[m]]} \) as the inner boundary and \( x=[[k]] \) as the outer boundary, the volume is \( \pi\int_{0}^{[[ytop]]}\left([[k]]^{2}-\dfrac{y}{[[m]]}\right)dy = [[answer]] \). (Using the shell method with respect to \( x \) instead, \( 2\pi\int_{0}^{[[k]]}x\cdot [[m]]x^{2}\,dx \), gives the same volume — either correct setup must agree.)
distractor_rationales: This treats the solid as a plain cylinder of radius \( [[k]] \) and height \( [[ksq]] \), forgetting to remove the inner region.
This uses the outer radius to the 4th power in place of integrating the varying inner boundary.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct volume.

stem: A region's upper boundary is the curve \( y=x^{2} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[p]] \), and then the constant \( y=[[p2]] \) for \( [[p]] \le x \le [[q]] \); its lower boundary is \( y=0 \) throughout. Because the upper boundary is defined piecewise, the correct setup is a SUM of two integrals (one per piece), not a single integral of \( x^{2} \) over the whole interval. Find the total area of this region.
solution: Splitting at \( x=[[p]] \), the area is \( \int_{0}^{[[p]]}x^{2}\,dx + \int_{[[p]]}^{[[q]]}[[p2]]\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly integrates \( x^{2} \) over the ENTIRE interval \( [0,[[q]]] \), ignoring that the boundary becomes constant after \( x=[[p]] \).
This only accounts for the first piece \( [0,[[p]]] \), dropping the rectangular piece entirely.
This only accounts for the second (rectangular) piece, dropping the curved piece entirely.
This doubles the correct total area.
This is off by one from the correct total area.
